title: New York Fed Report Shows Household Debt Edges Higher

source.name: Federal Reserve Bank of New York

summary: The New York Fed's Q3 2014 Household Debt and Credit Report shows total household debt increased $78 billion to $11.71 trillion, led by rises in auto loans, student loans, and credit cards. The report marks the apparent end of the household deleveraging period that followed the 2008 financial crisis, though total debt remains $970 billion below the Q3 2008 peak of $12.68 trillion.
key_points: Total household debt reached $11.71 trillion in Q3 2014, up $78 billion from Q2 2014 and $430 billion from Q3 2013
Auto loan originations hit $105 billion, the highest in nearly 10 years, with balances increasing for 14 straight quarters
Mortgage debt increased $35 billion to $8.13 trillion; HELOC debt decreased $9 billion, continuing a three-year decline
Overall 90-day delinquency rate fell to 4.3% from 4.5% in the prior quarter
Student loan delinquency rate reported at 11.1%, though actual rates likely higher due to deferment/forbearance masking
New foreclosures dropped to lowest level in the data series; approximately 113,000 individuals had new foreclosure notations
New York Fed economist Wilbert van der Klaauw stated the deleveraging period has ended and households are borrowing more
why_it_matters: Household debt trends are a key input for Fed assessment of financial stability and consumer spending capacity. The end of household deleveraging suggests potential support for consumption growth but also warrants monitoring for credit quality deterioration. This quarterly report serves as a baseline indicator for consumer credit market health.
macro_context: The report indicates consumer credit expansion with declining delinquency rates, suggesting households were managing debt responsibly as the economy recovered in late 2014. Rising auto and student loan balances reflected both demand and potential leverage in those sectors. This data was contemporaneous with ongoing Fed QE and near-zero rate policy.
market_structure_context: While not directly addressing market structure, household debt trends inform credit market conditions affecting mortgage-backed securities, consumer ABS, and broader financial stability assessments.

issues_found: Source is severely stale at 11+ years old; superseded by subsequent New York Fed household debt reports
market_impact.confidence is set to 0, which appears to be an incomplete/null error rather than a proper confidence assessment
asset_classes lists 'bonds' but the source does not discuss bond market implications—relationship to fixed income is indirect
topic is set to 'macro catalysts' but source metadata TARGET is 'market structure'; while macro catalysts is a valid scope, the classification could be tighter
Rule 7 exception for archival value applies but is borderline—the finding (end of deleveraging) is historically notable but the data has been updated in later quarterly reports

human_review.notes: The record is factually accurate and well-sourced, but requires human judgment on whether a 2014 baseline household debt report has sufficient archival value for a market structure/macro catalyst archive given its age and the availability of subsequent updated reports. Also needs clarification on market_impact confidence field (currently 0, appears incomplete).

notes: This New York Fed research report from November 2014 is historical and not directly relevant to market structure or macro catalysts. It provides baseline data on household debt trends during the post-crisis deleveraging period. Given the publication date is over 11 years prior to today (2026-04-10), it is classified as a stale historical record with low initial confidence. The topic field is set to 'macro catalysts' as a best-fit classification for economic data on credit conditions, though the source does not describe market-moving catalyst events.
